name: Division — Fair Sharing and Grouping
whatIsIt: Division splits a total into EQUAL parts. It answers two friendly questions: SHARING — '12 laddus shared by 3 friends, how many each?' — and GROUPING — '12 laddus packed 4 to a box, how many boxes?'. Both are written with the ÷ sign: 12 ÷ 3 = 4.
whyNeeded: Sharing fairly is one of life's most important jobs! Division makes sure everyone gets the same — no fights, no leftovers missed. It is also multiplication's twin: multiplication builds equal groups, division breaks a total back INTO equal groups. And fair sharing is exactly where fractions are born!
realLifeUses: Snacks: 12 laddus shared equally among 3 friends — 4 each
Teams: 20 children making 4 equal teams for kho-kho — 5 per team
Packing: 18 eggs packed 6 to a box — 3 boxes needed
Money: ₹50 shared equally between 2 siblings — ₹25 each
Car seats: 15 people, 5 seats per car — how many cars?

vocabulary.meaning: Splitting a total into equal parts: 12 ÷ 3 = 4.
Give everyone exactly the same amount.
Making groups of a fixed size and counting the groups.
What remains when things cannot be shared out evenly.

story.title: Sharing the Festival Laddus
story.text: Ajji has 12 laddus to share equally among her 3 grandchildren, so nobody grumbles. 'How many each?' She deals them one by one into 3 plates — fair sharing. Each plate ends with 4 laddus. 'Twelve shared among three is 12 ÷ 3 = 4 each. Think of the twin fact: 3 × 4 = 12. Fair sharing done!'
story.extractedProblem: 12 laddus are shared equally among 3 friends. How many laddus does each friend get?
story.answerInStory: 12 ÷ 3 = 4 — each friend gets exactly 4 laddus, and nobody grumbles!

visual.caption: 12 laddus dealt into 3 equal plates — 4 on each. Division makes equal groups: 12 ÷ 3 = 4.

teachingGallery.title: Division is fair sharing
teachingGallery.note: Share the whole into equal groups — how many in each?

examples.caption: Or count up in 4s to 12 → 3 jumps → 12 ÷ 4 = 3.

standardMethod.summary: Deal the total into equal shares (or equal groups), then count one share.
steps: Find the TOTAL (12 laddus) and the number of SHARES (3 friends).
Deal one to each share, again and again, until the total is used up.
Count how many landed in ONE share: 4.
Write the division sentence: 12 ÷ 3 = 4.

alternateMethods.name: Grouping method (measure out)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When you know the SIZE of each group and need the NUMBER of groups.
steps: Take out one group of the given size at a time: 12 laddus, boxes of 4.
Group 1 (4 gone, 8 left), group 2 (4 gone, 4 left), group 3 (empty!).
Count the groups you made: 3 boxes. So 12 ÷ 4 = 3.
alternateMethods.example: 18 eggs, 6 per box: take 6, take 6, take 6 — 18 ÷ 6 = 3 boxes.

alternateMethods.name: Think-multiplication method
alternateMethods.whenToUse: The fast mental method once tables are known — division is multiplication's twin!
steps: Turn 12 ÷ 3 into a missing-number multiplication: 3 × ? = 12.
Run the 3-times jumps: 3, 6, 9, 12 — that took 4 jumps.

alternateMethods.name: Array method
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To SEE both division questions in one picture.
steps: Arrange the total in an array: 12 dots in 3 rows.
Rows answer sharing: 3 friends → 4 in each row.
Columns answer grouping: groups of 3 → 4 groups.
One picture, two division stories!

workedExamples.problem: 20 children make 4 equal teams for kho-kho. How many children in each team?
steps: Total = 20, shares (teams) = 4.
Think multiplication: 4 × ? = 20.
Jump by 4: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20 — five jumps.
20 ÷ 4 = 5 children per team.

workedExamples.problem: 15 people are going by car, and each car seats 5. Ravi answers '15 ÷ 5 = 10 because 15 - 5 = 10.' Help him.
steps: Division is not take-away-once — it asks how many FIVES fit inside 15.
Measure out fives: 5 (one car), 10 (two cars), 15 (three cars).
Or think multiplication: 5 × 3 = 15.
15 ÷ 5 = 3 cars, not 10.
workedExamples.answer: 3 — division counts the equal groups, not one subtraction.

trickPractice.intro: Divide by counting how many equal groups fit — the twin of the times table.

hintLadder: How many groups of 3 fit in 12?
How many groups of 2 fit in 10?
How many groups of 5 fit in 20?
How many groups of 6 fit in 18?
How many groups of 4 fit in 24?
How many groups of 3 fit in 15?
How many groups of 8 fit in 16?
How many groups of 7 fit in 21?

commonMistakes.mistake: Handing out different amounts (7, 3, 2) and calling it sharing.
commonMistakes.fix: Fair sharing = SAME for everyone. Deal like cards — one each, round and round — until the pile is gone, then check all shares match.
Division keeps taking the group out until nothing is left, counting the groups: 5, 10, 15 → three groups. Or flip it: 5 × ? = 15.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing 3 ÷ 12 when 12 things are shared among 3 (order flipped).
commonMistakes.fix: The TOTAL always comes first: total ÷ shares. Twelve laddus shared by three friends is 12 ÷ 3.

easy.q: 8 chocolates are shared equally between 2 friends. How many does each friend get?
hintLadder: Deal them like cards: one for you, one for me...

easy.q: 12 laddus are shared equally among 3 friends. Which sentence is right?
hintLadder: Which number is the TOTAL? That one comes first.

medium.q: 18 eggs are packed 6 to a box. How many boxes are filled?
hintLadder: This is grouping: take out 6 at a time.
How many sixes fit in 18? Think 6 × ? = 18.

medium.q: ₹50 is shared equally between 2 siblings. How many rupees does each get?
hintLadder: Half of 50 — split it into two equal piles.

challenge.q: Ravi shares 12 laddus among 3 friends as 7, 3, and 2. 'Done — division!' he says. What would Divya Divider say?

options.label: Not division — the shares must all be EQUAL

challenge.answer: Not division — the shares must all be EQUAL
hintLadder: The total is right... but check the plates.
Does everyone have the same amount?
Division = equal shares only. Deal one-each, round and round.

challenge.q: A school van seats 8 children. 24 children are going on a trip. Multi asks the twin question: 8 × ? = 24. How many vans are needed?

questions.q: 10 pencils are shared equally between 5 children. How many pencils each?
What must be true about the shares in division?

options.label: The first person can take more

hintLadder: Same amount for every share — always.

questions.q: 21 mangoes are packed 7 to a basket. How many baskets?
hintLadder: Grouping: how many sevens fit in 21?

questions.q: 16 biscuits shared among 4 friends. Which division sentence is correct?

hintLadder: How many jumps did that take?

teachBackPrompt: Divya Divider is training new fairness helpers! Teach someone at home the two division questions — sharing ('how many each?') and grouping ('how many groups?') — using 12 of anything. Show the card-dealing trick, and tell them which number always comes first in the division sentence.

revisionCard.summary: Division = the fairness machine. Total ÷ shares, total always FIRST. Two questions: sharing (12 ÷ 3 friends = 4 each) and grouping (12 ÷ 4 per box = 3 boxes). Twin trick: 12 ÷ 3 = ? is 3 × ? = 12. Equal shares only!

gameMission.title: The Fairness Machine
gameMission.brief: The party guests are waiting! Deal laddus, mangoes, and pencils into perfectly equal shares before Ravi's grabby paws reach the box. Bonus round: pack the delivery boxes using the grouping method.

realLifeProject: At snack time, take 12 of something small (grapes, biscuits, peanuts) and share them equally among family members — deal like cards! Write the division sentence you performed. Then try 13 and discover what a 'left over' is!

meta.curriculum: CBSE/NCERT Class 3 (division as sharing and grouping)

mentalMathMethod.name: Think the multiplication twin
steps: Ask: what times the divisor makes this number?
Use the times table you know.
That missing factor is the answer.

abacusMethod.name: Share beads into equal groups
steps: Set the total in beads.
Deal them out equally into the number of groups.
Count how many each group got.

visual.caption: Share the beads equally — 4 in each group.

rememberIt.hook: Division = sharing equally OR making equal groups
rememberIt.unpack: 12 / 3: share 12 into 3 equal groups (4 each), or how many 3s fit in 12 (4). Remainder = leftovers.

studentQuestions.q: What are the two meanings of division?
studentQuestions.a: Sharing equally (12 sweets among 3 kids = 4 each) OR grouping (how many 3s in 12 = 4).
What is left over when it does not divide evenly. 13 / 4 = 3 remainder 1.
